quotes.verbatim_quote:
When going to a higher string, use a down-stroke.
   When going to a lower string, use an up-stroke.
   When playing on one string, use alternate strokes.
quotes.why_load_bearing: These three rules are the foundational thesis of the entire book — every exercise downstream is an application of this picking economy principle.

quotes.verbatim_quote: I discovered when two notes were not on the same string, the pick had to travel past the string that the second note was on and then reverse direction to come back up and play the note. That is a long way for the pick to travel. I found one concept or rule that causes the pick to travel the least amount of distance.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Establishes the underlying economy-of-motion rationale that justifies why directional picking replaces strict alternate picking.

quotes.verbatim_quote: This technique creates a significant change in one’s right hand technique or, I prefer the term “habit”; most guitarists have been using the alternate picking “habit” for many years. That habit is not easy to break. With a fair amount of practice and discipline you can break that habit and develop a much more efficient right hand technique.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Frames the method as a deliberate replacement for an entrenched habit, setting expectations that practice will feel awkward before it pays off.

quotes.verbatim_quote: The right hand, wrist and elbow should always be relaxed and never tense or tight. The wrist should not move or tilt. The movement should come from the elbow. Do not touch the strings, bridge or any other part of the guitar with the fingers of your right hand. Your palm may rest lightly on the strings.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Defines the physical mechanics Bruno's picking system depends on — elbow-driven motion with a still wrist is a precondition for the directional strokes to work.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
Down-up: Everyone can do this one
        Up-down: A bit more difficult, especially keeping the accent strong. The
           goal is to make the up-stroke sound as strong and round as the down-
           stroke.
        In-between – stroke: This stroke requires the pick to move between two
           adjacent strings or non-adjacent strings. This stroke is difficult to master
quotes.why_load_bearing: Names and ranks the stroke vocabulary used throughout the book, telling the student which strokes need the most practice attention.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
Consecutive down-stroke and consecutive up-stroke: When practicing this
         technique it is important to play the notes evenly and with an equal
         amount of attack. The tendency is to rush the notes that follow the initial
         stroke. An example would be two, three or four notes, each on an adjacent
         string, played with a single down or up stroke depending on the direction
         of the phrase. The consecutive up-stroke is harder to control.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Identifies the specific rhythm-evenness failure mode (rushing) that arises from sweeping multiple adjacent strings with one stroke direction.
The notation key that every subsequent exercise in the book depends on for decoding picking direction.

quotes.verbatim_quote: I recommend that you practice all the exercises starting with both strokes. There should be only a very slight difference in sound.
quotes.why_load_bearing: A practice-rule that generalizes every exercise: each must be drilled from both an up-start and a down-start to internalize symmetry.

quotes.verbatim_quote: I recommend you practice these exercise at MM 60 to 120, then increase your speed from there.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Gives the concrete metronome range that anchors how the student should work through every exercise in the book.

quotes.verbatim_quote: The exercises are designed to take the student from point A to Z. It is not a good idea to skip around in this book
quotes.why_load_bearing: Declares the book's curriculum as ordered — a usage rule telling the reader not to cherry-pick exercises.
